one_liner: A 24-year-old previously healthy man presents with progressive dyspnea, fatigue, and palpitations 2 weeks after a flu-like illness with fever, myalgias, and diarrhea.

stage2.hpi_extension: Two weeks ago he had 5 days of fever, muscle aches, sore throat, and watery diarrhea that resolved on its own. Over the past 5 days he has developed exertional dyspnea, fatigue, and occasional palpitations. Yesterday he had a syncopal episode while walking to the bathroom. He denies chest pain radiating to the arm or jaw, no cough, and no tobacco or drug use.
stage2.social_history: Graduate student. Non-smoker, occasional alcohol, no IV drugs. Recently returned from a camping trip.
stage2.physical_exam: Young man appearing mildly ill and fatigued. Cardiac: tachycardic with S3 gallop; soft holosystolic murmur at the apex. JVD present. Lungs: bibasilar crackles. Abdomen: mild hepatomegaly. Extremities: trace pitting edema; cool extremities with delayed capillary refill. No rash. Tachycardia out of proportion to fever.

lab_results.result: Troponin I elevated at 2.1 ng/mL (no coronary risk factors). CK-MB elevated. ESR 60, CRP 8.5 (elevated). BNP 980 pg/mL. Viral PCR panel pending.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Troponin elevation without CAD risk factors in a young patient after a viral illness suggests myocarditis. BNP reflects acute systolic dysfunction.

lab_results.result: Transthoracic echocardiogram: globally hypokinetic LV with EF 30%, mild LV dilation, no valvular disease, small pericardial effusion. Cardiac MRI with gadolinium: patchy mid-wall and epicardial late gadolinium enhancement with myocardial edema on T2-weighted imaging — findings consistent with acute myocarditis.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Global hypokinesis with mid-wall/epicardial LGE (non-coronary distribution) on cardiac MRI is the imaging gold standard for myocarditis and distinguishes it from ischemic cardiomyopathy.

lab_results.result: Sinus tachycardia. Diffuse low-voltage QRS complexes. Nonspecific ST-T changes. Occasional PVCs. No Q waves.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Low voltage and nonspecific ST-T changes are typical of myocarditis; frank ST elevations may also occur but usually in a non-coronary distribution.

stage5.final_clue: Viral PCR returns positive for Coxsackievirus B; endomyocardial biopsy (done for refractory arrhythmias) shows a lymphocytic infiltrate with myocyte necrosis.

debrief.classic_presentation: Myocarditis classically presents in a young, previously healthy patient with new heart failure symptoms, chest pain, arrhythmias, or sudden cardiac death, typically preceded by a viral prodrome (flu-like illness, gastroenteritis) 1–2 weeks earlier. Exam shows tachycardia out of proportion to fever, S3 gallop, and signs of systolic heart failure. It is a major cause of sudden cardiac death in young adults.
debrief.pathophysiology: Myocarditis is inflammation of the myocardium, most often due to viral infection with Coxsackievirus B (most common in developed countries), adenovirus, parvovirus B19, HHV-6, and others. In South America, Trypanosoma cruzi (Chagas disease) is the classic cause. Direct viral injury and post-infectious T-cell mediated immune response cause myocyte necrosis. Histology: lymphocytic infiltrate with myocyte necrosis on endomyocardial biopsy (Dallas criteria). Can progress to dilated cardiomyopathy.
debrief.treatment_overview: Management is supportive: guideline-directed heart failure therapy (ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics), arrhythmia management, and restriction from strenuous exercise for 3–6 months. Mechanical circulatory support (ECMO, LVAD) or transplant for fulminant disease. Avoid NSAIDs (may worsen myocardial inflammation). Immunosuppression is reserved for specific types (giant cell myocarditis, eosinophilic myocarditis, autoimmune). Most cases resolve within weeks to months.
high_yield_pearls: Coxsackievirus B is the most common cause of viral myocarditis in developed countries; in South America think Chagas disease (T. cruzi) which causes megacolon, megaesophagus, and dilated cardiomyopathy.
Histology: lymphocytic infiltrate with myocyte necrosis on endomyocardial biopsy (Dallas criteria).
Myocarditis is a major cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes — restrict from exercise for 3–6 months.
Look for tachycardia out of proportion to fever and a viral prodrome 1–2 weeks earlier in a young patient with new heart failure symptoms.
Cardiac MRI shows patchy mid-wall/epicardial late gadolinium enhancement in a non-coronary distribution, distinguishing myocarditis from ischemic injury.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Cardiovascular chapter, 'Cardiomyopathies — Myocarditis' section.
